accomplishments: Create a button that responds when you click it
Write your first line of JavaScript code

do.title: Make a Button Say Hello
steps: Open VS Code (or go to CodePen.io in your browser)
Create a new file and save it as talking-button.html
Copy and paste the code below
Double-click the file to open it in your browser
Click the button — you'll see a popup appear!
do.code: My First JavaScript
do.result: You just created an interactive button that responds to clicks using JavaScript!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the popup message "Hello! You clicked me!" after clicking your button.

concepts.name: The <button> tag + onclick
concepts.definition: The button creates a clickable element. The onclick attribute tells it what JavaScript to run when clicked.
concepts.analogy: A doorbell button on a house. onclick is the wiring that connects it to the chime.

concepts.name: alert() function
concepts.definition: JavaScript code that shows a popup message. Every browser understands alert(). Great for testing, not great for real websites (popups are annoying).
concepts.analogy: A sticky note that pops up in front of everything until you press OK.

concepts.name: HTML creates elements, JavaScript makes them DO things
concepts.definition: HTML builds the structure (the button). JavaScript adds behavior (what happens when you click). They work together but are different languages.
concepts.analogy: HTML is building a car. JavaScript is the engine that makes it drive.

understand.keyTakeaway: HTML creates elements. JavaScript makes them DO things. onclick + alert() is the simplest way to start.

build.goal: Make the button show a personalized greeting with YOUR name

codeComparison.new:
<button onclick="alert('Hello, Sarah! Welcome to JavaScript!')">Click Me!</button>

<!-- Replace "Sarah" with YOUR actual name! -->

build.hint: Make sure to keep the quotes around your text: alert('like this'), not alert(like this). The quotes tell JavaScript this is text to display.

challenge.title: Create a Compliment Button
challenge.mission: Create a button that gives you a different compliment each time you click it, picked randomly from 3 options.
requirements: Button text says "Give Me a Compliment"
When clicked, shows one of 3 different compliments
Each compliment is encouraging and positive
challenge.starterCode: Daily Compliment Generator
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add 5 compliments instead of 3! Change the random number generator to pick from 1-5.

capabilities: Use onclick to make elements respond to clicks
Use if/else statements to make decisions

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to change what's on the page WITHOUT annoying popups. You'll make text appear, disappear, and change — all with JavaScript.

accomplishments: Make text on your page change when you click a button
Create a name tag that updates in real-time
Change multiple things on the page at once

do.title: Make a Headline Change
steps: Create a new file and save it as changing-text.html
Copy and paste the code below
Click the button — watch the headline change instantly!
do.code: Hello World
do.result: The headline text changed WITHOUT reloading the page. That's JavaScript manipulating the DOM!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the headline AFTER clicking (should say "I just changed this with JavaScript!")

concepts.name: The id attribute
concepts.definition: Gives an HTML element a unique name (like a name tag). Every id on a page must be unique. In JavaScript, you find it with #id.
concepts.analogy: A student ID number. No two students have the same one.

concepts.name: document.querySelector()
concepts.definition: JavaScript's way of saying "go find this element on the page." Use #id for IDs, .class for classes. Returns the first matching element.
concepts.analogy: A search function — you describe what you're looking for, it finds it and hands it to you.

concepts.name: .textContent
concepts.definition: Changes the text inside an element. Whatever you put after = becomes the new text. Doesn't change HTML structure, just the visible text.
concepts.analogy: Erasing what's written on a whiteboard and writing something new.

concepts.name: The = sign in JavaScript
concepts.definition: In JavaScript, = means "assign this value" (not "equals" like math). x = 5 means "make x equal 5." To check equality, use === instead.
concepts.analogy: Putting something INTO a box (=) vs. checking what's IN the box (===).

understand.keyTakeaway: Select an element with querySelector(), then change it with .textContent. That's the core pattern for DOM manipulation.

build.goal: Create a name tag that updates with YOUR name
build.hint: Keep the quotes around your name: 'Sarah' not Sarah. Without quotes, JavaScript thinks you're talking about a variable.

challenge.title: Build a Mood Ring
challenge.mission: Create three buttons that change a text display to show different moods with emojis.
requirements: One text area that displays the current mood
Each button changes the text + emoji
The mood text should be big and centered
challenge.starterCode:
How are you feeling?

Click a button to set your mood
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add two more mood buttons: "Excited" and "Calm" with their own emojis!

capabilities: Change text on a page without reloading
Use .textContent to update what elements say
Give elements IDs and select them with #
Create multiple functions that change the same element
Build interactive interfaces that respond to user actions

bridge.preview: In the next lesson, you'll learn how to change not just the TEXT, but the STYLE — colors, sizes, backgrounds. You'll build a theme switcher that transforms your entire page.
